description: A shoulder meets a stuck door, and the door moves away from the contact. 'pesa' sends force through that pressure and away from it; a button or control can be pressed this way even when it hardly moves. 'natu' (pull) draws through a held connection, while 'peki' (squeeze) brings pressure inward from more than one side.
articulatory_notes: Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Raise the tongue blade near the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 's', send air through its central groove, and finish with open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The lips hold and release the opening 'p'. The narrow stream of 's' carries the breath onward before open 'a', which can make the form feel like pressure breaking into motion.
usage_notes: The thing under pressure is the direct object. A directional phrase can give its course, and 'kema' (strong) before the verb makes the manner forceful. When a kick drives something away, the foot is the instrument of the push.

semantic_domains.activity: applying force through pressure against a person or thing
semantic_domains.physical: outward force transmitted through direct contact
semantic_domains.spatial: movement away from the point of pushing contact
